query: What is the punishment for dowry demand in India?

data.answer:
According to the context, the punishment for giving or taking dowry is imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than 5 years, and with a fine which shall not be less than 15,000 rupees or the amount of the value of such dowry, whichever is more. (Context 1)

However, the Court may, for adequate and special reasons to be recorded in the judgment, impose a sentence of imprisonment for a term of less than 5 years. (Context 1)

NOTICE: The Act does not specifically mention the punishment for dowry demand. It only mentions the punishment for giving or taking dowry. 

However, according to the context, the burden of proof in certain cases lies on the person who is being prosecuted for taking or abetting the taking of any dowry. (Context 4)

Practical next step: If you or someone you know is a victim of dowry demand, please report it to the authorities immediately. You can also contact the National Commission for Women (NCW) for assistance and support.

Agreement for giving or taking dowry to be void.—Any agreement for the giving or taking of dowry shall be void. 6. Burden of proof in certain cases.—Where any person is prosecuted for taking or abetting the taking of any dowry under section 3, or the demanding of dowry under section 4, the burden of proving that he had not committed an offence under those sections shall be on him.] 1. [Page 1] 1 THE DOWRY PROHIBITION ACT, 1961 ACT NO. 28 OF 1961 [20th May, 1961.] An Act to prohibit the giving or taking of dowry. BE it enacted by Parliament in the Twelfth Year of the Republic of India as follows:— 1. Short title, extent and commencement.—(1) This Act may be called the Dowry Prohibition Act, 1961. (2) It extends to the whole of India except the State of Jammu and Kashmir. (3) It shall come into force on such date1 as the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint. 2. Definition of “dowry”.—In this Act,
